title: Symplectic fillability of contact graph manifolds via line arrangements
speaker: Laura Starkston
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Convex fillings, concave caps, and plumbing sign criteria
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up a contact 3-manifold via its symplectization, distinguishes convex symplectic fillings from concave caps, and recalls that negative-definite plumbings give convex boundaries while suitably positive plumbings give concave boundaries.

proof_stages.label: Seifert-fibered concave caps as the motivating classification problem
proof_stages.summary: For Seifert fibered spaces over S^2, a star-shaped plumbing with a central +1 sphere gives a fixed concave cap and a natural contact structure, so classifying convex fillings becomes the problem of understanding possible complementary symplectic pieces.

proof_stages.label: Reduction of fillings to symplectic line arrangements and blow-ups
proof_stages.summary: Any filling embeds as the complement of the chosen cap, and the possible cap embeddings are obtained from symplectic line arrangements in CP^2 by blowing up prescribed incidence points, splitting the classification into incidence combinatorics and isotopy classes of geometric realizations.

proof_stages.label: Branched-cover obstruction to realizing projective-plane arrangements
proof_stages.summary: For the Fano-plane incidence pattern, and more generally for finite-projective-plane-type patterns, blowing up all multiple points and using mod-p homology relations to form branched covers gives Euler-characteristic and signature calculations incompatible with the negative spheres that would lift, ruling out even topological realizations in CP^2.

proof_stages.label: Contact graph manifolds from abstract incidence data
proof_stages.summary: Starting with any combinatorial line arrangement, even one not realizable in CP^2, one builds a plumbing-like four-manifold deformation retracting to +1 spheres with the prescribed incidences, and the Li-Mak positivity criterion supplies a concave symplectic structure whose boundary is a contact graph manifold.

proof_stages.label: Fillability equivalent to symplectic realizability
proof_stages.summary: A convex filling of the contact graph manifold can be glued to the concave cap, and McDuff’s classification forces the resulting closed symplectic manifold to be CP^2 in a way that recovers a symplectic realization of the incidence data; hence nonrealizable arrangements yield nonfillable contact manifolds.

proof_stages.label: Universal tightness via Colin gluing
proof_stages.summary: The explicitly constructed boundary contact manifold decomposes into universally tight pieces glued along incompressible pre-Lagrangian tori, so Colin’s gluing theorem implies the resulting graph-manifold contact structures are universally tight, producing tight but nonfillable examples.

examples.name: Previously classified fillings of S^3 and lens spaces
examples.note: The three-sphere and lens spaces are cited as familiar contact 3-manifolds whose convex symplectic fillings had already been classified, positioning Seifert fibered and graph-manifold cases as the next step.
examples.engagement_reason: The prior fillings of S^3 and lens spaces are cited as known classification results motivating the problem, with no computation or application to those cases.

passes.reason: S^3 and lens spaces are low-complexity spherical/Seifert base cases cited as prior filling classifications, not used to refute a claim.
These are the simplest sphere/cyclic lens-space filling cases, cited as prior classifications rather than as refutations.
The S^3 and lens-space cases are highly regular low-complexity base cases used as prior classified benchmarks.
S^3 and lens spaces are the lowest-complexity/base cases in the filling-classification story rather than adversarial examples.
S^3 and lens spaces are the low-complexity spherical/base cases cited as prior classification benchmarks, not as failures of a claim.
S^3 and lens spaces are low-complexity spherical/base cases for filling classifications, cited only as prior benchmarks.
used as prior classified low-complexity base cases, with S^3/lens spaces much simpler than general contact graph manifolds
Cited as prior base cases for filling classification, with S^3/lens-space boundaries serving as low-complexity minimal cases rather than refuting a claim.
Used as previously solved benchmark cases; S^3 and lens spaces are low-complexity spherical/base-case contact 3-manifolds.
S^3 and lens spaces are highly regular spherical contact-manifold cases cited as prior classification context, not to refute a claim
They are cited as historical baseline cases, with S^3 and lens spaces being highly regular spherical/quotient contact 3-manifolds.
used only as prior base cases; S^3 and lens spaces are low-complexity spherical manifolds with high regularity, not obstructions.

examples.name: Pappus-type non-algebraic symplectic perturbation
examples.note: A Pappus theorem configuration is used to show, by local perturbation, a simple but dissatisfying way that symplectic line configurations can violate an algebraic incidence forced for complex projective lines.
examples.engagement_reason: The speaker draws/describes the Pappus configuration and explains concretely how a local symplectic perturbation breaks the forced third collinearity to produce a non-algebraic symplectic arrangement.

passes.reason: The Pappus-type symplectic perturbation is used to violate the algebraic Pappus collinearity condition, giving a non-algebraically realizable symplectic arrangement.
The Pappus-type arrangement has special collapsed multiple incidences and is used to show that symplectic arrangements need not be algebraically realizable.
The Pappus-type symplectic perturbation has special collapsed incidence combinatorics and is used to show algebraic realizability/Pappus behavior can fail symplectically.
the Pappus-type perturbation is used to exhibit a symplectic line arrangement that cannot be realized algebraically.
The Pappus-type symplectic arrangement has collapsed multi-incidence combinatorics and is used to show symplectic arrangements need not be algebraically realizable.
The Pappus-type symplectic arrangement has special collapsed incidence constraints and is used to show algebraic/Pappus realizability can fail symplectically.
the perturbed Pappus-type symplectic arrangement violates algebraic Pappus realizability and has special collapsed incidence/triple-point combinatorics
Used to show algebraic realizability can fail, via a Pappus-type arrangement with special multiple-incidence degeneracies perturbed symplectically.
Used to violate algebraic Pappus collinearity/stretchability; the local symplectic perturbation is not sporadic, extremal, or maximally regular.
The Pappus-type symplectic arrangement uses special multiple-incidence combinatorics to violate algebraic/Pappus realizability
It is used to show that a symplectic line arrangement need not be algebraically realizable, and its Pappus-type incidence has nongeneric multiple-point coincidences.
the perturbed Pappus incidence is used to show symplectic line arrangements need not be algebraically realizable, and it is a deliberately non-generic multiple-incidence arrangement.

examples.name: Real projective pseudoline arrangements as possible sources
examples.note: Topologically embedded line arrangements in the real projective plane are mentioned as a possible source of symplectic sphere arrangements in CP^2 with non-algebraic combinatorics, illustrating open directions rather than proving the main result.
examples.engagement_reason: Real projective pseudoline arrangements are proposed as possible future sources of non-algebraic symplectic configurations, but no specific arrangement is analyzed or constructed in detail.

passes.reason: Real projective pseudoline arrangements are mentioned only as a broad possible future source of non-algebraic symplectic configurations, not as an established counterexample here.
Real projective pseudoline arrangements are invoked only as a speculative general source of examples, not as an established obstruction or sporadic special object.
Real projective pseudoline arrangements are mentioned as a prospective source of nonalgebraic symplectic configurations, not yet as a deployed refutation.
real projective pseudoline arrangements are proposed as a general source of future examples, not as an established obstruction or counterexample here.
Real projective pseudoline arrangements are a broad topological family proposed as possible sources, not a specific established counterexample here.
Real projective pseudoline arrangements are invoked as a broad ordinary source class for future constructions, not as established obstructions or failures.

Presented only as speculative sources for nonalgebraic symplectic arrangements; pseudoline arrangements form a broad ordinary topological class.

They are proposed only as work-in-progress sources of candidates, and pseudoline arrangements form a systematic topological family rather than a structurally special object.
